# Tool System

The tool system is one of the core abstractions in the project. It is how the model asks the runtime to do work.

## The Contract

`Tool.ts` defines the shared shape for every tool:
- name and description
- input and output schema
- validation and permission rules
- rendering and progress behavior
- tool-use context and callbacks

The use of Zod schemas is important. Tool input and output are not treated as loose JSON blobs; they are typed contracts that keep the model boundary explicit.

## Registry

`tools.ts` builds the global tool list. It mixes always-on tools with feature-gated tools and environment-gated tools. That registry is the source of truth for what the model can actually call in the current runtime.

The registry is split by capability, not by file type:
- file and shell tools
- agent delegation tools
- task and planning tools
- web and MCP tools
- special Ant-only or feature-flagged tools

## Delegation

`tools/AgentTool/AgentTool.tsx` is the main delegation primitive. It can spawn subagents, route work to specialized agent definitions, and create background or isolated execution paths.

That tool is more than a convenience wrapper. It is the mechanism that turns the app into a multi-agent system.

## Tool Behavior Model

Tools can be:
- read-only
- concurrency-safe
- deferred until turn boundaries
- permission-gated
- backgroundable

This lets the runtime distinguish quick local actions from operations that need full coordination or user approval.

## Command vs Tool

Commands are user-facing slash actions. Tools are model-facing capabilities. The two are related but not identical. A command may invoke a tool, and a tool may reveal command-like behavior, but the contract boundaries are different.
